question: Why was George W. Bush's official re-election website blocked for users outside the United States?
answer: The blocking was the result of a deliberate policy decision by the Bush campaign, not due to any attack by hackers or vandals. Starting in the early hours of Monday in October, non-US browsers received an 'unauthorised' message. Net monitoring firm Netcraft detected the block from its stations in London, Amsterdam, and Sydney, while its four US-based stations could access the site normally. The campaign began using Akamai's content delivery services around the same time. Netcraft president Mike Prettejohn speculated the decision was taken to cut costs and traffic in the run-up to the November election, though this also meant Americans living overseas were inadvertently blocked.

question: How does geographic IP blocking work, and how could some users bypass the Bush campaign website block?
answer: Geographic blocking works because numerical IP addresses used to organise the internet are assigned on a regional basis, allowing servers to identify the approximate location of a visitor. Some users could bypass the block by using anonymous US-based proxy services. Additionally, some web users in Canada reported being able to access the site normally.

question: What did the Conservative Party propose regarding local policing accountability in the UK?
answer: Conservative Party leader Michael Howard proposed that local communities would elect their own Area Police Commissioner. This new role would replace existing police authorities (which were made up of local councillors and magistrates) while not superseding the role of a chief constable. Howard argued elected commissioners would be more visible and accountable than the largely unknown police authorities. Other Tory law and order plans included building more prisons and requiring criminals to serve full jail sentences.

question: What were the main criticisms raised against the Conservative plan for elected police commissioners?
answer: Critics raised several objections. The Police Federation of England and Wales chair Jan Berry warned it could result in those with extreme political views dictating policing on the ground. Liberal Democrat spokesman Mark Oaten called the plan dangerous, saying it could create conflict between chief constables and elected officials, and suggested a better approach would be for elected councillors to draw up a minimum policing guarantee with chief constables. Labour criticised the Tories for making extravagant promises without explaining how they would be funded beyond 'fantasy savings' on asylum. Lord Harris of the Association of Police Authorities warned it would overturn nearly 200 years of keeping policing decisions free from political interference.

question: What is the UK government's revised policy on storing foreign nuclear waste, and how is it justified financially?
answer: The UK government confirmed that intermediate-level waste (ILW) from countries including Japan, Germany, Spain, Italy, Switzerland, and Sweden, which was previously contracted to be shipped back to its country of origin, will now be stored permanently in the UK. Only highly radioactive waste will continue to be returned under armed guard. The policy change is expected to raise up to £150 million for Britain's nuclear clean-up programme under the new Nuclear Decommissioning Agency. The government argued it would result in a sixfold reduction in waste shipments and that highly radioactive waste would be returned sooner, ensuring no overall increase in radioactivity.

question: How did the Liberal Democrats respond to the UK government's decision to store foreign nuclear waste permanently?
answer: Lib Dem environment spokesman Norman Baker strongly condemned the policy, accusing ministers of turning Britain into a 'nuclear dumpsite'. He said he had been warning about this for months and had raised it with the government several times. Baker argued that Britain's environmental and health needs were being ignored in favour of policies driven by the Treasury and Department of Trade and Industry, calling it a terrible attempt to offload some of the £48 billion cost of cleaning up nuclear sites. He described the nuclear industry as an 'economic, social, and environmental millstone' around Britain's neck.

question: What was Germany's unemployment rate in February and what was the significance of the figure?
answer: Germany's unemployment rate reached more than 5 million people, or approximately 12.6% of the working-age population, the highest jobless rate in Europe's biggest economy since the 1930s. A government economic adviser, Bert Ruerup, warned that the earlier growth forecast of 1.5% was too optimistic and revised it down to just 0.5% for the year.

question: How did Germany's Hartz IV programme aim to address unemployment?
answer: The Hartz IV programme, introduced in January, aimed to tackle stubbornly high unemployment levels through a range of labour market reforms centred on shaking up welfare benefits and pushing people back into work, even if some of the jobs were heavily subsidised. The German government hoped these reforms would help reduce the persistently elevated unemployment figures.

question: How did a change in methodology affect the reported German unemployment figures?
answer: A change in the methodology used to measure unemployment pushed up the jobless rate by more than 400,000 in January. Adjusted for seasonal changes, the overall unemployment rate was 4.8 million people, or 11.4%, up 0.4 percentage points from the previous month. Using the internationally accepted ILO methodology, Germany had 4.3 million people out of work in January, and ILO-based figures also suggested that 30,000 new net jobs were created that month, bringing total employment to 38.8 million.

question: What caused the Jakarta stock market to fall after the Asian tsunami?
answer: The Jakarta Stock Exchange saw its biggest slide in a month after Indonesia doubled its estimate of the likely cost of rebuilding from the Asian tsunami. The government said it expected debt repayments of up to 32 trillion rupiah (£1.9bn) to be frozen to help pay for the recovery. Construction, property, and banking shares were among the main affected sectors.

question: What debt relief arrangements were discussed for Indonesia following the tsunami?
answer: Indonesian economy minister Aburizal Bakrie stated that the Paris Club group of creditor nations was expected to freeze approximately 9.5 billion rupiah in debt payments due in 2005 and 2006, with repayments expected to resume from 2007. French finance minister Hervé Gaymard confirmed that the Paris Club had already agreed to a moratorium on repayments for tsunami-hit countries ahead of its formal meeting on 12 January.

question: Why did Japanese shares reach their highest level since July 2003, and what was the Nikkei predicted to reach?
answer: Japanese shares ended the year at their highest since July 2003 amid hopes of economic recovery in 2005. Morgan Stanley equity strategist Naoki Kamiyama predicted the Nikkei index would rise toward 13,000 points. Traders expected strength in the global economy to benefit Japan, alongside structural reforms and an anticipated end to the banking sector's bad debt problems.

question: What factors dampened business confidence in Japan according to the Tankan survey?
answer: The quarterly Tankan survey of Japanese manufacturers found that business confidence had weakened for the first time since March 2002. The fall was attributed to slower economic growth, rising oil prices, a stronger yen, and weaker exports. Japan had been close to sliding into recession in recent months.

question: What did InBev's acquisition of Alfa-Eco's stake in Sun Interbrew mean for the company's control of the Russian brewer?
answer: The deal gave InBev, the world's biggest beermaker, near-total control over Sun Interbrew, Russia's second-largest brewer. InBev agreed to buy Alfa-Eco's one-fifth stake for up to 550 million euros. Following the deal, InBev would own 91% of the voting shares and 89% of the non-voting shares of Sun Interbrew. InBev had already bought out another partner in August 2004.

question: Where does Sun Interbrew operate its breweries across Russia and Ukraine?
answer: Sun Interbrew owns breweries in eight Russian cities: Klin, Ivanovo, Saransk, Kursk, Volzhsky, Omsk, Perm, and Novocheboksarsk. The company also operates three breweries in Ukraine, located in the cities of Chernigov, Nikolaev, and Kharkov.

question: What prompted the UK-wide civil servants' strike in Wales and how significant was it?
answer: The strike was called by the Public and Commercial Services (PCS) union in protest at planned job cuts announced by Chancellor Gordon Brown, who said 104,000 jobs would be cut with around 16,000 of those expected in Wales — the worst-affected area in the UK. The action was the biggest by civil servants in a decade, with thousands joining pickets across towns including Cardiff, Bangor, Aberystwyth, and Wrexham.

question: What was Gordon Brown's response to the civil servants' strike?
answer: Gordon Brown issued a defiant statement saying the strike action would not affect the government's determination to make savings in order to increase investment in healthcare, education, transport, and the fight against crime. He said the government's decisions would mean more police, teachers, doctors, and nurses, and pledged help with information, relocation, and retraining to move staff into frontline public sector roles, but declared the government would not be diverted from the changes.

question: Why was the civil service strike in Wales considered especially significant?
answer: Wales has a proportionally higher number of civil servants than any other part of the UK. PCS union spokesman Jeff Evans noted that the civil service is a major employer in Wales, and the cuts would particularly affect Objective One areas and Welsh-speaking communities. A striker at Companies House in Cardiff explained that the majority of colleagues were not coming in due to threats to their jobs and concerns about maintaining face-to-face public services, particularly for pensioners.

question: How dramatically did phishing email attacks grow in 2004 according to MessageLabs?
answer: MessageLabs reported that phishing email attacks grew more than tenfold in less than 12 months. In September 2003, the firm caught only 279 phishing emails, but by September 2004 it was stopping more than two million phishing-related email messages per month. Over the full year 2004, MessageLabs detected more than 1.2 million phishing email messages.

question: How were phishing attacks evolving in sophistication by the end of 2004?
answer: Phishing attacks were becoming increasingly sophisticated. While older attacks relied on users failing to notice that a website was fake, more recent phishing emails attempted to steal details as soon as the message was opened. Some scams sought to recruit innocent people as middlemen for laundering money or goods bought with stolen credit cards. MessageLabs CTO Mark Sunner warned that attacks were shifting from a random 'scattergun' approach to highly customised, focused attacks targeting specific businesses and organisations, with some companies being threatened and blackmailed.

question: What proportion of all email was spam by the end of 2004?
answer: By the end of 2004, almost three-quarters of all email messages were spam. This represented a dramatic rise: in 2003, only 40% of messages were spam. One in every 16 messages was also infected with a virus.

question: How does BT's ArtGarden search technology allow users to browse the Tate's online collection?
answer: ArtGarden, developed by BT's research unit, uses a system called 'smart serendipity' — a combination of artificial intelligence and random selection — to help users browse paintings without needing to know an artist's name or title. Users are shown a selection of pictures, and clicking their favourite changes the displayed gallery to a selection of visually or thematically similar works. The system scores paintings based on keywords, both obvious ones (such as the artist's name or subject) and broader thematic or stylistic terms, then combines keyword matching with an element of chance.

question: What practical experience inspired the development of ArtGarden?
answer: The idea grew from the personal frustration of Richard Tateson, who worked on the ArtGarden project. He tried to shop for his wife's Christmas present at an online clothes store but found the text-based search — restricted to searching by garment type or designer — unhelpful. He ended up shopping on the high street instead. This experience led him to question why text-based searching dominated online discovery when browsing by feel or look might be more intuitive for many shoppers.

question: What future applications did BT plan to explore for the ArtGarden smart search technology?
answer: BT Research was looking at extending the smart serendipity search technology beyond art to other media, including searching for music and films. The Tate's digital programme head Jemima Rellie also expressed hope that the ArtGarden technology could be incorporated into the Tate website in the near future.

question: What key trends in casual gaming were identified by industry experts ahead of the 2005 Consumer Electronics Show?
answer: Experts predicted that easy-to-play casual games — playable online or downloadable to mobile devices — would see strong growth in 2005. The panel noted that casual gaming was not just for women, as commonly assumed; titles like poker, pool, bridge, bingo, and puzzle games were described as gender neutral. The trend also showed that even hardcore gamers sought casual experiences when not playing blockbuster titles. Portal sites like AOL, Yahoo, and RealArcade were seen as key enablers, allowing players to build buddy lists and access games cheaply without long time commitments.

question: According to AOL's research, what differences existed between male and female casual gamers in terms of game preferences?
answer: AOL's program director Greg Mills said its research showed that sports-based casual games attracted 54% of 18-to-34-year-old males, while puzzle games were played by 74% of females. Games like bridge tended to attract the over-50 demographic of gamers.

question: What vision did Geoff Graber of Yahoo Games have for the future of casual gaming?
answer: Geoff Graber, director of Yahoo Games (which attracted about 24 million gamers a month), believed casual games would get into their stride in 2005, be really big in 2006, and by 2007 would be defined by community, with more people finding more to do with their gadgets and high-speed connections. He saw casual games opening up gaming as a mass-market form of entertainment. He and the panel agreed that the immediate focus should be attracting third-party developers to offer more innovative and multiplayer titles.

question: What was France's digital terrestrial television service called, and what did the name stand for?
answer: France's digital terrestrial TV service was officially called TNT, which stands for Télévision Numérique Terrestre (Digital Terrestrial Television). It was being branded publicly as Télévision Numérique pour Tous, meaning 'digital television for all'. TNT was a joint venture between public broadcaster France Télévisions and a handful of cable and satellite operators.

question: How popular was the UK's Freeview compared to Sky Digital, and how did this influence France's DTT launch?
answer: Figures from UK regulator Ofcom showed Freeview was more popular than Sky Digital satellite service in the three months to September 2004, with almost five times as many people signing up to the free-to-air DTT service compared with Sky. Almost half of UK households had gone digital on at least one TV set through cable, satellite, or Freeview. TNT's chief operating officer cited this success, along with positive experiences in Italy and Germany, as evidence that DTT was a very good idea and that France — one of Europe's poorest markets in terms of free-to-air channels — had strong potential.

question: What were the launch coverage targets for France's TNT digital terrestrial service?
answer: TNT aimed to reach 35% of France's population at launch from 115 transmission sites. The initial transmission area would include Paris, Lille, Lyon, and Toulouse. Eventually there would be 1,626 sites reaching over 85% of homes. The CSA regulator said the goal of reaching 85% of the population by 2007 was achievable, though coverage for the remaining viewers in mountainous or border areas would require public sector action.

question: What were analysts predicting about the impact of free DTT on pay-TV growth in France?
answer: Some analysts were pessimistic about pay-TV prospects, predicting that consumers would be less likely to sign up for pay-TV subscriptions once free DTT launched. Henri de Bodinat, vice-president of Arthur D. Little consultancy, said they believed free DTT could put the brakes on the underlying growth of pay-TV in France. TNT's own chief Gerolami, however, remained optimistic that it would 'revolutionise television in France'.

question: Why did Cat Deeley leave her role as host of ITV's CDUK?
answer: Cat Deeley resigned after six years as host of ITV's Saturday morning children's music show CDUK to concentrate on new television projects. The 27-year-old presenter said she'd had a great few years, met incredible people, and been given wonderful opportunities, but expressed excitement about the future. Guest presenters were to stand in until a replacement was found after her final show on 5 March.

question: What notable TV achievements had Cat Deeley accomplished before leaving CDUK?
answer: Before leaving CDUK, Cat Deeley had co-hosted SMTV, the children's show that aired before CDUK on Saturday mornings, alongside Ant and Dec. She also hosted Stars in Their Eyes. She won a Children's BAFTA award for Best Presenter in December 2000 and fronted BBC One's Fame Academy series.

question: How did Desperate Housewives help Channel 4's ratings in January?
answer: The debut of US hit Desperate Housewives helped lift Channel 4's January audience share by 1% compared to the previous year. The comedy drama managed to pull in five million viewers at one point during its run, attracting a quarter of the television audience. Combined with successes like Celebrity Big Brother and The Simpsons, Channel 4 surpassed BBC Two's audience share for the first month since the previous July.

question: What was the state of digital multichannel TV growth in the UK during early 2005?
answer: Digital multichannel TV was the strongest area of growth in UK television. The BBC reported Freeview box ownership of five million, including one million sales in the last portion of 2004. Freeview's share of the audience soared by 30% in January 2005 compared with the previous year. Meanwhile, both BBC One/Two and ITV saw their monthly audience shares decline year-on-year for January.

question: Why was Dame Julie Andrews' attendance at the Mary Poppins stage show significant?
answer: It was the first time Dame Julie Andrews had seen the musical staged since she had made the original 1964 Disney film. She watched Laura Michelle Kelly reprise the nanny role at the Prince Edward Theatre in London's West End. The visit was also meaningful because the theatre was where Dame Julie had her first starring role, in a performance of Humpty Dumpty in 1953.

question: How much did the stage production of Mary Poppins cost to bring to the West End and who were its creative leads?
answer: The Mary Poppins musical cost £12 million to bring to the stage. It was masterminded by theatre impresario Sir Cameron Mackintosh and directed by Richard Eyre, with choreography by Matthew Bourne. The show had already won two Olivier Awards, including Best Musical Actress for Laura Michelle Kelly and Best Choreography.

question: Why was the Lord of the Rings musical premiering in Toronto rather than London?
answer: The producers abandoned plans for a London premiere because no suitable West End theatre was available — all three West End theatres with sufficient capacity were already booked. The £12 million show was therefore set to debut in Toronto in March 2005 at the Princess of Wales Theatre, co-produced by Canadian theatrical impresarios David and Ed Mirvish. A London opening was not expected before December 2006.

question: Who was composing the music for the Lord of the Rings stage musical and what style was it intended to be?
answer: The music was being written by Bollywood composer AR Rahman, who also worked on Andrew Lloyd Webber's West End musical Bollywood Dreams, in conjunction with Finnish group Varttina. Producer Kevin Wallace assured there would be no singing and dancing hobbits — the music would be in a traditional mould drawing on ethnic traditions. The production was described as a hybrid of text, physical theatre, music, and spectacle.

question: What role did Jimmy Connors plan to play in developing British tennis talent?
answer: Former world number one Jimmy Connors was planning a long-term relationship with the Lawn Tennis Association (LTA) to help develop the next generation of British players. He spent three days at the LTA's annual elite performance winter camp in La Manga and expressed desire to be involved for a number of weeks per year rather than a one-off short-term arrangement. LTA chief executive John Crowther said Connors had already built a good relationship with coaches and players and they hoped it was the beginning of a lasting partnership.

question: What impressed Jimmy Connors most about the British junior tennis players he worked with at La Manga?
answer: What impressed Connors most was not only the quality of the coaches but the way the young players, some as young as 12 or 13, approached their workouts — the feeling and desire they put into every practice session. He said their passion and desire could only come from coaches instilling the importance of work and practice, adding that such dedication was something you cannot buy.

question: What additional role did Jimmy Connors agree to take on for the BBC?
answer: Jimmy Connors agreed to commentate for the BBC at the following year's Wimbledon Championships, working during the second week of the tournament.

question: How did Roger Federer defeat Ivan Ljubicic in the Dubai Championship final?
answer: Federer won the Dubai final in three sets. He dominated the first set, winning it comfortably, but Ljubicic dug deep to win the second set in a tense tiebreak. Federer responded by turning on the style to win the deciding set. The match was a repeat of the previous week's final at the World Indoor Tournament in Rotterdam, which Federer had also won, though Ljubicic had stretched him to five sets on that occasion.

question: What did Ljubicic say about his poor first-set performance against Federer in Dubai?
answer: Ljubicic was at a loss to explain it. He said he hadn't started badly but suddenly felt like his racket was loose and the balls were flying too much. He noted that against Federer, if you relax for a second, games go very quickly. He described it as 'really weird' given that he had been playing well all year, and said after those first three games it was 'no match at all'.

question: What positive takeaway did Ljubicic draw from his defeat in the Dubai final?
answer: Despite losing, Ljubicic was pleased with his overall performance. He noted that he'd had a chance to win in the third set and viewed the result positively, saying it was a great boost to his confidence that he was 'up there and belongs with top-class players', having reached back-to-back finals against Federer in two consecutive weeks.

question: What was Marat Safin's emotional state before and during the 2005 Australian Open final against Lleyton Hewitt?
answer: Safin admitted he was nervous and couldn't play tennis in the early stages. He was mentally burdened by memories of his two previous unsuccessful Australian Open finals in 2002 and 2004. He said he was thinking about those finals when he lost the opening set to Hewitt, struggling with pressure because 'you want to win' so badly at that stage. He noted that Hewitt, having more experience, appeared less nervous.

question: How did Safin's 2005 Australian Open semi-final win over Roger Federer affect his confidence for the final?
answer: Safin said his semi-final win over world number one Federer gave him huge confidence going into the final. He explained that once he beat Federer and then Hewitt in the final, it was like getting so much self-belief that you realise you can play great tennis, win big titles, and beat huge players in finals and semi-finals.

question: Was Jonny Wilkinson fit to play for England against Ireland in the 2005 Six Nations?
answer: No. According to his Newcastle club boss Rob Andrew, Wilkinson was not fit for England's Six Nations match in Dublin on 27 February. Andrew said there was 'no doubt' he wouldn't be ready for Dublin, though he might recover in time for the later matches against Scotland and Italy. Wilkinson had not played for England since the 2003 World Cup final due to a succession of injuries.

question: What problems were England's kickers facing in the 2005 Six Nations, and what was Rob Andrew's assessment?
answer: England had wasted a 17-point half-time lead in their 18-17 defeat to France after goalkickers Charlie Hodgson and Olly Barkley missed six penalty attempts and a drop-goal between them. Rob Andrew acknowledged that both Hodgson and Barkley were among the best kickers in the Premiership but said the problem was handling pressure, and that England were 'just not handling the pressure at the moment'.

question: What mental skills did former England fullback Dusty Hare say were essential for a top goalkicker?
answer: Hare said that alongside technical practice, natural-born mental toughness was crucial. A top goalkicker must be able to shut out all outside elements and concentrate solely on putting the ball between the posts. He noted that Charlie Hodgson had begun incorporating crowd noise into his practice routine to try to simulate match pressure, comparing the challenge to top golfers who also must manage their mental game. Hare concluded that great goalkickers like Jonny Wilkinson 'come along very rarely'.

question: What did Chelsea's statement say about their drug policy when they sacked Adrian Mutu?
answer: Chelsea's statement declared that the club has a 'zero tolerance policy towards drugs', applying equally to performance-enhancing drugs and so-called recreational drugs. The club stated that such substances 'have no place at our club or in sport' and that in coming to their decision they believed the club had a social responsibility to its fans, players, employees, and other stakeholders in football.

question: What was Adrian Mutu's form like at Chelsea before his drugs test failure?
answer: After arriving at Stamford Bridge, Mutu scored six goals in his first five games. However, his form then went into a decline and he was frozen out by coach José Mourinho before the drugs test failure led to his sacking.

question: What was Yelena Isinbayeva's world record achievement at the Birmingham indoor athletics meet, and why was the event considered disappointing overall?
answer: Yelena Isinbayeva set another world pole vault record at the meet. However, the achievement could not hide that the event was lacklustre overall. Despite her record, observers felt it was not enough to lift the tone of the entire meeting, which was widely regarded as underwhelming.

question: What was Cristiano Ronaldo's position on signing a new contract with Manchester United?
answer: Ronaldo said he was close to agreeing to a new contract at Old Trafford and expressed confidence it would be resolved soon, saying he thought they would 'reach a good agreement for both sides'. However, he also refused to fully commit his long-term future to the club, saying nobody knew what could happen in the future.

question: What was the stated purpose of the European digital lifestyle collaboration involving Nokia, Philips, Intel, Alcatel, and France Telecom?
answer: The consortium of companies aimed to shape how people receive and interact with digital entertainment in the future. The collaboration sought to enable people to locate digital content they desire and have it delivered seamlessly when on the move, regardless of device or network. The goal was to establish common standards for digital content delivery across Europe, with competing companies working together to avoid fragmented, incompatible platforms.

question: What was the Electronic Frontier Foundation's legal argument in defence of online journalists sued by Apple?
answer: The EFF argued that the online journalists were protected by the American Constitution and that Apple's attempt to subpoena their ISPs for email records was an attack on press freedom. The EFF's lawyer Kurt Opsahl stated that by going directly to the journalists' ISPs for email records, Apple was undermining constitutional protections. The EFF joined the journalists' legal team to fight the case head-on.

question: What civil dispute was taking place within India's Reliance Industries group?
answer: A family feud was unfolding between the two Ambani brothers who ran Reliance Industries, India's largest conglomerate. Anil Ambani, the younger brother, was losing his position at a Reliance unit. His elder brother Mukesh controlled Reliance Industries itself, which in turn owned Indian Petrochemicals Corporation (IPC). The brothers' dispute had dominated Indian headlines, with Mukesh having spoken of restructuring the group.

question: What were Jonny Wilkinson's main leadership qualities cited after England's Six Nations struggles?
answer: Rob Andrew identified Wilkinson, along with Martin Johnson and Lawrence Dallaglio, as crucial leadership losses for England. He argued that their retirements and injuries left the side without the leadership needed to see them through difficult moments. Jason Robinson was mentioned as struggling to lead effectively from fullback. Dusty Hare also highlighted Wilkinson's approximately 80% kicking success rate and his ability to be a 'cool customer' under pressure as key qualities England were sorely missing.

question: What was Charles Kennedy's central charge against Tony Blair ahead of the 2005 general election?
answer: Lib Dem leader Charles Kennedy said voters now had a fundamental question of trust about Tony Blair, particularly in the context of the Iraq war. Kennedy appeared before a studio audience and covered Lib Dem tax plans, anti-terror laws, and immigration. He argued that the world had changed out of recognition and that the case for the Iraq war was no longer being defended.

question: What was the purpose of the election-focused pressure campaign described in the article about what the election 'should really be about'?
answer: The article described a campaign by pressure groups to put their priority issues — such as poverty, pensioner income, and inequality — at the centre of the general election debate. One in five pensioners was cited as living below the poverty line, and the campaign aimed to publish results by constituency and nationally to hold politicians to account. The general election was seen as the best chance for most pressure groups to influence political priorities, with the campaign designed to make results visible and put leaders under public pressure.

question: What happened on the first day of the UK hunting ban coming into force?
answer: Thousands of hunt supporters went out across Britain on the first day of the Hunting Act ban, with more than 250 hunts participating on the opening day. Hunts argued they were operating within the law by using alternatives such as drag hunting or trail hunting, since chasing and shooting animals were still legal. One anti-hunt protester was taken to hospital after a violent clash in Kent. Some hunts reportedly faced potential prosecution under new poaching laws.

question: What novel won the US National Book Prize for fiction and what did the author use the occasion for?
answer: A novel set in 19th-century Paraguay won the fiction prize at the US National Book Awards. The author used the award ceremony to speak out about censorship. Sales of the winning author's books had exceeded a certain million figure, indicating a popular as well as critically acclaimed writer. The honorary prize went to someone notable for both popular success and literary greatness.

question: What disciplinary action did David Prutton of Southampton face from the Football Association?
answer: Southampton's David Prutton faced a possible seven-match ban when he appeared before the Football Association. The charge against him was for using threatening words and/or behaviour towards a match official during a league draw. The precedent cited was Paolo Di Canio receiving a seven-match suspension for pushing referee Paul Alcock.

question: What was José Mourinho's reaction to learning no FA action would be taken against him following comments about referees?
answer: The FA confirmed they had looked at Mourinho's comments and decided no further action was required, calling it the end of the matter. In response, Mourinho mused that things might be 'a few things' different — perhaps when he turned 60 and had been managing in the same league for 20 years and had the same record as other managers, people might see him differently.

question: What was the US economy's job creation figure for October and how did it compare to expectations?
answer: The US economy added 337,000 jobs in October, a seven-month high that was far more than analysts had expected. The unemployment rate itself ticked up slightly, from 5.4% in September, because more people were actively seeking work. The strong jobs data had an upward effect on New York stocks, with the Dow Jones index gaining around 100 points to reach approximately 10,360 by late morning.

question: What was the outcome of Tottenham's League Cup tie against Newcastle under Martin Jol?
answer: Tottenham defeated Newcastle to advance to the quarter-final of the League Cup. Manager Martin Jol praised his players for their patience in the win, noting that Newcastle had a very good side and were always difficult. Jol expressed confidence that Spurs could go to Newcastle and get a win in the next round.

question: What was Roger Federer's reflection on his Dubai final against Ljubicic?
answer: Federer said he really wanted to get off to a good start and was able to play with confidence while Ljubicic was still looking for his rhythm. He described how he almost ran away with the match before Ljubicic came back, acknowledging it was 'a good effort' on Ljubicic's side. Federer remained composed throughout and won the deciding set to claim the title.
